content:
# Luke Chapter 16 — Translation Disagreements

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Translators:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Verse-by-Verse Disagreements

### 16:1 — Opening phrase
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And he was saying also to the disciples" |
| LOGOS | "And he said also to his disciples" |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus also said to his disciples" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"And he said also to his disciples"** |

**Issue:** EMET preserves the imperfect tense ΕΛΕΓΕΝ ("was saying"); LOGOS normalizes to simple past; PNEUMA supplies "Jesus" (not in Greek) and drops the connective "And."
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The imperfect is standard Greek narrative introduction and does not require "was saying" in English. PNEUMA's insertion of "Jesus" is interpretive.

---

### 16:1 — "steward" vs. "manager"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "steward" |
| LOGOS | "steward" |
| PNEUMA | "manager" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"steward"** |

**Issue:** ΟΙΚΟΝΟΜΟΣ can be rendered either way. "Manager" is more contemporary; "steward" carries theological weight.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1). "Steward" preserves the concept of stewardship central to the parable's theology.

---

### 16:2 — "Render" vs. "Give back" vs. "Turn in"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Give back the account" |
| LOGOS | "Render the account" |
| PNEUMA | "Turn in a complete account" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Render the account"** |

**Issue:** ΑΠΟΔΟΣ ΤΟΝ ΛΟΓΟΝ — EMET is literal ("give back"), LOGOS formal ("render"), PNEUMA dynamic ("turn in a complete account," adding "complete").
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Render" is the precise formal equivalent in accounting contexts.

---

### 16:3 — "strong enough" vs. "able"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "I am not strong-[enough] to dig" |
| LOGOS | "I am not able to dig" |
| PNEUMA | "I am not strong enough to dig" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"I am not strong enough to dig"** |

**Issue:** ΙΣΧΥΩ means "to have strength/be strong," distinct from ΔΥΝΑΜΑΙ ("to be able"). LOGOS conflates the two verbs.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA's sense — "strong enough" preserves the distinct vocabulary of ΙΣΧΥΩ, but without EMET's brackets.

---

### 16:6 — "written bond" vs. "written-document" vs. "bill"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "written-document" |
| LOGOS | "written bond" |
| PNEUMA | "bill" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"written bond"** |

**Issue:** ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΑ — three different register levels. "Written-document" is over-hyphenated; "bill" is too casual; "written bond" is appropriately formal.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS for register consistency.

---

### 16:8 — "praised" vs. "commended"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "praised" |
| LOGOS | "commended" |
| PNEUMA | "commended" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"commended"** |

**Issue:** ΕΠΗΝΕΣΕΝ — "praised" and "commended" are both valid. "Commended" carries a nuance of professional approval that suits the master-steward relationship.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1).

---

### 16:8 — "toward their own generation" vs. "in dealing with their own kind"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "toward their own generation" |
| LOGOS | "in relation to their own generation" |
| PNEUMA | "in dealing with their own kind" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"in dealing with their own generation"** |

**Issue:** ΕΙΣ ΤΗΝ ΓΕΝΕΑΝ ΤΗΝ ΕΑΥΤΩΝ — EMET preserves "toward" (directional); LOGOS uses "in relation to"; PNEUMA replaces "generation" with "kind."
**Resolution:** SOPHIA combines PNEUMA's natural "in dealing with" with the literal "generation" from EMET/LOGOS, preserving ΓΕΝΕΑΝ.

---

### 16:9 — "by means of" vs. "out of"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "out of the mammon of unrighteousness" |
| LOGOS | "by means of the mammon of unrighteousness" |
| PNEUMA | "use worldly wealth" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"by means of the mammon of unrighteousness"** |

**Issue:** ΕΚ ΤΟΥ ΜΑΜΩΝΑ — EMET renders ΕΚ as "out of"; LOGOS as "by means of" (instrumental); PNEUMA paraphrases entirely, dropping "mammon" and "unrighteousness."
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The instrumental sense is primary here. PNEUMA's "worldly wealth" loses the Aramaic loanword and the quality-genitive.

---

### 16:10 — "He who is faithful" vs. "The [one who is] faithful" vs. "Whoever is faithful"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "The [one who is] faithful in [the] least [thing]" |
| LOGOS | "He who is faithful in a very little thing" |
| PNEUMA | "Whoever is faithful with very little" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"He who is faithful in a very little thing"** |

**Issue:** Ο ΠΙΣΤΟΣ ΕΝ ΕΛΑΧΙΣΤΩ — three different approaches to the substantive adjective. EMET's brackets are distracting; PNEUMA's "with" replaces the Greek ΕΝ ("in").
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS for clean formal English.

---

### 16:14 — "sneering at" vs. "scoffing at"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "sneering-at" |
| LOGOS | "scoffing at" |
| PNEUMA | "sneering at" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"sneering at"** |

**Issue:** ΕΞΕΜΥΚΤΗΡΙΖΟΝ — "sneering" (from nose-wrinkling) vs. "scoffing" (vocal contempt). The Greek verb derives from ΜΥΚΤΗΡ ("nose").
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1). "Sneering" better preserves the nasal etymology.

---

### 16:16 — "proclaimed as good news" vs. "proclaimed as good tidings"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "is being-proclaimed-as-good-news" |
| LOGOS | "is proclaimed as good tidings" |
| PNEUMA | "the good news of the kingdom of God is being proclaimed" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"is proclaimed as good news"** |

**Issue:** ΕΥΑΓΓΕΛΙΖΕΤΑΙ — EMET hyphenates excessively; LOGOS uses archaic "good tidings"; PNEUMA restructures the sentence.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "proclaimed as good news" — combining LOGOS's structure with modern "good news" over archaic "good tidings."

---

### 16:18 — "Everyone who divorces" vs. "Everyone divorcing" vs. "Anyone who divorces"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Everyone divorcing his wife and marrying another commits-adultery" |
| LOGOS | "Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery" |
| PNEUMA | "Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another woman commits adultery" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery"** |

**Issue:** ΠΑΣ Ο ΑΠΟΛΥΩΝ — EMET preserves the participial form; LOGOS and PNEUMA use relative clauses. PNEUMA adds "woman" (not in Greek).
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The relative clause is natural English.

---

### 16:19 — "Now" vs. "And"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And a certain man was rich" |
| LOGOS | "Now there was a certain rich man" |
| PNEUMA | "There was a rich man" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Now there was a certain rich man"** |

**Issue:** Narrative transition marker. EMET preserves ΔΕ as "And"; LOGOS uses "Now" to mark the new parable; PNEUMA drops the connector entirely.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Now" marks the shift to the second parable, consistent with SOPHIA's practice at 15:25.

---

### 16:21 — "longing" vs. "desiring"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "desiring to be fed" |
| LOGOS | "longing to be fed" |
| PNEUMA | "longing to eat what fell" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"longing to be fed"** |

**Issue:** ΕΠΙΘΥΜΩΝ — "desiring" is more neutral; "longing" captures the sustained yearning of the participle.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1), consistent with the choice at 15:16.

---

### 16:22 — "to the bosom of Abraham" vs. "to Abraham's side"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "into the bosom of Abraham" |
| LOGOS | "to the bosom of Abraham" |
| PNEUMA | "to Abraham's side" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to the bosom of Abraham"** |

**Issue:** ΕΙΣ ΤΟΝ ΚΟΛΠΟΝ ΑΒΡΑΑΜ — "bosom" preserves the banquet-reclining imagery (ΚΟΛΠΟΣ); "side" (PNEUMA) loses this resonance.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Bosom" retains the theologically significant imagery of intimate closeness at a feast.

---

### 16:23 — "in his bosom" vs. "in his bosoms" vs. "at his side"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in his bosoms" (plural, literal) |
| LOGOS | "in his bosom" (singular) |
| PNEUMA | "at his side" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"in his bosom"** |

**Issue:** ΕΝ ΤΟΙΣ ΚΟΛΠΟΙΣ ΑΥΤΟΥ — Sinaiticus reads plural. EMET preserves the plural literally; LOGOS normalizes to English singular; PNEUMA paraphrases.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. English idiom uses singular "bosom"; the plural is a Greek stylistic variant without distinct theological significance here.

---

### 16:24 — "having cried out" vs. "having called-out"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "having called-out" |
| LOGOS | "having cried out" |
| PNEUMA | "called out" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"having cried out"** |

**Issue:** ΦΩΝΗΣΑΣ — "called out" is neutral; "cried out" conveys urgency and anguish appropriate to the context.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The context of torment warrants the more urgent "cried out."

---

### 16:25 — "received your good things" vs. "received-in-full your good [things]"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "received-in-full your good [things]" |
| LOGOS | "received your good things" |
| PNEUMA | "received your good things" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"received your good things"** |

**Issue:** ΑΠΕΛΑΒΕΣ — EMET renders the ΑΠΟΛΑΜΒΑΝΩ "in-full" nuance explicitly; LOGOS and PNEUMA render simply as "received."
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1). The "in-full" sense is theologically important but is better addressed in notes than interpolated into the translation.

---

### 16:28 — "warn" vs. "testify"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "warn" |
| LOGOS | "testify to" |
| PNEUMA | "warn" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"warn"** |

**Issue:** ΔΙΑΜΑΡΤΥΡΗΤΑΙ — compound verb with intensified ΜΑΡΤΥΡΕΩ. "Testify" preserves the root meaning; "warn" captures the contextual urgency.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1). In context, the purpose is clearly admonitory — the rich man wants his brothers warned away from his fate.

---

### 16:29 — "Abraham said" vs. "Abraham says"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Abraham says" (historical present) |
| LOGOS | "Abraham said" |
| PNEUMA | "Abraham replied" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Abraham said"** |

**Issue:** The Greek may use a historical present (ΛΕΓΕΙ) which EMET preserves. LOGOS and PNEUMA normalize to past tense.
**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. Consistent past-tense narration is standard SOPHIA practice; the historical present is a Greek narrative device that creates inconsistency when preserved in English.

---

## Summary Statistics

| Category | Count |
|---|---|
| Total verses | 31 |
| Full agreement (all three translators) | 11 |
| Minor disagreements (word choice/register) | 14 |
| Significant disagreements (meaning/structure) | 6 |
| SOPHIA followed LOGOS | 18 |
| SOPHIA followed EMET | 5 |
| SOPHIA followed PNEUMA | 2 |
| SOPHIA followed EMET+LOGOS (2-to-1) | 3 |
| SOPHIA followed LOGOS+PNEUMA (2-to-1) | 5 |
| SOPHIA followed EMET+PNEUMA (2-to-1) | 4 |
| SOPHIA hybrid/independent | 3 |

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA.*
